name: MacTutor
biography:
Fritz ( Friedrich ) B횄쩌tzberger attended primary school in his native village of Bleienbach and secondary school in Langenthal. He attended the Gymnasium in Burgdorf. In 1880 he began his studies at the Engineering Department of the Polytechnic, but after a year he transferred to the Department for Mathematics and Physics Teachers. He graduated from the Polytechnic in 1884 and began working as a mathematics teacher at his former secondary school in Langenthal, after having worked at a school in Solothurn for a few months.
Whilst teaching in Langenthal he also studied towards a doctorate at the University of Bern. He completed his thesis Ein mit der Theorie algebraischer Fl횄짚chen zusammenh횄짚ngendes planimetrisches Problem 창혫혟 ( A problem associated with the planimetric theory of algebraic surfaces ) in 1888 . The doctorate was conferred "summa cum laude", with highest honours [ 8 ] . B횄쩌tzberger was the 11 th of Schl횄짚fli 's 12 doctoral students.
B횄쩌tzberger became a mathematics teacher at the Kantonsschule in Z횄쩌rich in 1896 . From 1899 onwards he taught in the school's technical track only, thus preparing future engineers for their university studies. Furthermore, he also taught mathematics at Z횄쩌rich's adult education centre. At the University of Z횄쩌rich he delivered lectures on descriptive geometry to future secondary school teachers. In 1903 he took up a teaching post at the technical school in Burgdorf.
B횄쩌tzberger was a 'first-rate teacher and author of several much valued textbooks with ample exercises' [ 9 ] . His textbooks include Lehrbuch der ebenen Trigonometrie mit vielen Aufgaben und Anwendungen 창혫혟 ( Textbook of plane trigonometry with many problems and applications ) ( Z횄쩌rich, 4 th edition 1910) , Lehrbuch der Stereometrie 창혫혟 ( Textbook of solid geometry ) ( Z횄쩌rich, 3 rd edition 1916) , and Lehrbuch der Arithmetik und Algebra f횄쩌r Mittelschulen 창혫혟 ( Textbook of arithmetic and algebra for middle schools ) ( Z횄쩌rich, 2 nd edition 1920) . All of them were reviewed favourably and were reprinted several times. Salkowski calls Lehrbuch der Stereometrie 창혫혟 ( Textbook of solid geometry ) an 'established book' [ 10 ] , whilst Barneck comments on B횄쩌tzberger's style in Lehrbuch der Arithmetik 창혫혟 ( Textbook of arithmetic and algebra for middle schools ) ...: 'The presentation is broad and comprehensible for pupils' [ 5 ] . Zacharias commends Lehrbuch der ebenen Trigonometrie 창혫혟 ( Textbook of plane trigonometry ) ... for similar reasons [ 11 ] :- The [ writing ] is succinct and clear; the definitions and theorems are succinct and easy to learn throughout; historic remarks make for interesting reading; numerous exercises, partly  theoretical, partly practical, are included in the individual sections [ ... ] Moreover, B횄쩌tzberger published several papers, among them Eif횄쨋rmige Drehk횄쨋rper 창혫혟 ( Egg-shaped rotating bodies ) (1917) , which is aimed at secondary school pupils. In this paper he determines centroids of ovoids, using the Guldinus theorem. As he mentions in the introduction, 'one of my pupils, a keen ornithologist, inspired me to solve [ the problems treated here ] ' [ 6 ] . B횄쩌tzberger also wrote a very readable biography of the Bernese mathematician Georg Sidler [ 7 ] , his friend and former teacher. In 1912 he gave a talk at the annual meeting of the Swiss Mathematical Society .
  He had a particular interest in Jakob Steiner and published a couple of biographical papers on the geometer. More importantly, he organised and edited Steiner 's papers from 1823 - 26 on the request of the Bernese Society for Natural Scientists. As B횄쩌tzberger remarks in [ 3 ] , Graf discovered Steiner 's handwritten manuscripts covering the period from 1814 - 1826 in the attic of the Town Library in Bern. Graf then passed the documents on to B횄쩌tzberger 'to put them in order and to good use' [ 3 ] . The collection of B횄쩌tzberger's papers is now stored in the ETH Library Archive.
More details of B횄쩌tzberger's work on Steiners papers are at THIS LINK B횄쩌tzberger joined the organising committee of the first International Congress of Mathematicians ( or enlarged committee as it is called in the minutes ) in December 1896 . At his first committee meeting on 8 December he joined the reception committee chaired by Hurwitz . At the meeting on 31 July 1897 , Rudio suggested that a 'correspondence and mail room should be established and a postal service organised' [ 4 ] ; B횄쩌tzberger accepted this task.
B횄쩌tzberger was married to Rosa Kohler. The couple had two children: a son, Fritz, and a daughter, Marie. There is little information about B횄쩌tzberger's personal life in the obituaries and his estate. In a letter to U Hoepli and his wife in 1920 [ 2 ] , B횄쩌tzberger mentions his son and daughter. Presumably he would have mentioned any further children. He also refers to his brother Ernst and his brother-in-law Hardmeyer.   Ulrico Hoepli (1847 - 1935) was a Swiss bookseller who emigrated to Milan. He became one of Italy's most important publishers, specialising in sciences and Italian classics, and an influential art patron.
